title: City of Berkeley Adopted Biennial Budget FY2025-FY2026
council_members_note: All members listed voted for a budget that explicitly disclosed one-time balancing measures and acknowledged they were not sustainable.

verbatim_non_sustainability_admission.quote: While these strategies lead to a balanced biennial budget, they are not sustainable and efforts to either increase revenues and/or decrease expenditures beyond the baseline budget will need to be undertaken as part of the FY26 Mid-Biennial Budget Update and as part of the budget development process for fiscal years 2027 and 2028.
verbatim_non_sustainability_admission.scoring_significance: City Manager explicitly told council the FY2025-2026 budget strategies were not sustainable at the time of adoption. All council members who voted yes were on notice.

verbatim_expenditures_outpace_revenues.quote: As expenditures outpace revenues, the biennial budget required the prioritizing of resources...
verbatim_expenditures_outpace_revenues.scoring_significance: The revenue-expenditure gap is named in the first paragraph of the budget message — not buried. Every council member received this document before voting.

one_time_measures_disclosed_at_adoption.description: The budget message explicitly lists four one-time balancing measures used to achieve a balanced FY2025-FY2026 budget.
measures: (1) Increase in assumed salary savings materializing from vacant positions
(2) Not making any funding allocations to the workers compensation fund in either fiscal year
(3) Utilizing $3 million of funding from the City's Section 115 Trust toward pension expenses in each fiscal year, thereby allowing more General Fund to be redirected toward other expenditures
(4) The use of approximately $2-3 million of fund balance (prior years' savings) in FY25 and FY26 respectively
one_time_measures_disclosed_at_adoption.scoring_significance: These are the same four measures the City Auditor flagged in the April 2026 financial condition report as one-time strategies being used to mask a structural deficit. The council approved a budget that disclosed them explicitly and acknowledged they were not sustainable.

key_financial_figures.gf_fund_balance_use_note: Apparent $12.3M shortfall reduced to ~$2.8M actual draw due to salary savings and Section 115 Trust offset.

pension_cost_jump.verbatim: The leading drivers of expenditure growth include pension and health insurance, with pension increasing by nearly $10 million, or 23%, in FY25 when compared to FY24 and healthcare up by $4 million, or 20% respectively.
pension_cost_jump.scoring_significance: A 23% pension cost jump in a single year was known at budget adoption. The council approved no structural response — instead using the Section 115 Trust drawdown to cushion the impact.

arpa_cliff.status: FY25 marked the exhaustion of remaining unspent ARPA funding that had previously been used to balance FY2022-2024.
arpa_cliff.scoring_significance: The ARPA cliff was a known and disclosed revenue removal. No recurring replacement revenue was identified at adoption.

revenue_volatility_disclosed.property_transfer_tax: Volatile and declining — 'significantly declined with decreased home sales, largely attributed to high interest rates.'
revenue_volatility_disclosed.verbatim: some of our leading, but volatile, General Fund revenue, such as property transfer tax, have significantly declined with decreased home sales, largely attributed to high interest rates.

staffing_and_vacancies.vacancy_savings_note: Budget relies on assumed salary savings from vacancies as a balancing mechanism — explicitly noted as not sustainable in the conclusion.

conclusion_verbatim: On the expenditure-side, maintaining salary savings due to vacancies is not sustainable over the long-term, the City's three pension plans are not 100% funded and personnel costs related to pension are anticipated to increase, inflation is at an all-time high and the City's infrastructure needs are great.
scoring_implications: Every council member who voted for the FY2025-2026 budget received a document stating expenditures outpace revenues, listing four one-time balancing measures, and saying those measures were 'not sustainable.' There is no basis for claiming ignorance of the structural deficit.
Pension costs jumped 23% in FY25. The council's response was to draw from the Section 115 Trust rather than restructure spending. The drawdown was disclosed at adoption.
Workers' comp fund received zero allocation in both FY25 and FY26 — a deferred obligation, not a savings. The City Auditor later flagged this as a one-time measure masking structural imbalance.
ARPA funds ($66M total) were fully exhausted as of FY25 with no recurring revenue replacement identified.
The all-funds budget draws $118M of fund balance in FY25 alone — 15% of expenditures. The budget message itself calls this 'not sustainable.'
